meta.title: Why do rockets scrub because of weather?
meta.description:
If you’ve ever watched a rocket launch live, whether in person, or online, you’ve likely heard the range be talked about by the hosts. The Range is in charge of monitoring the exclusion zones, weather and they're even who has their hand on the button for Flight Termination System if things go wrong!

This video is thanks to The  @U.S.SpaceForce who sponsored this video!

00:00 - Intro
01:12 - What is the Range
08:09 - Weather
14:02 - Weather Balloons
16:12 - Exclusion Zones
24:55 - Flight Termination
32:07 - Tracking
34:50 - Summary

--------------------------

I'm finally on Nebula! Sign up today at go.nebula.tv/everydayastronaut

Want to support what I do? Consider becoming a Patreon supporter for access to exclusive livestreams, our discord channel! - http://patreon.com/everydayastronaut

Or become a YouTube member for some bonus perks as well! - https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC6uKrU_WqJ1R2HMTY3LIx5Q/join

All music is original! Find it anywhere you listen to music! (Spotify, iTunes, Google Play, Amazon, etc)

transcript.text: Welcome to Vandenberg Space Force Base.
Today, I'm going to be taking you guys
inside of Vandenberg to get [music] some
exclusive, never-beforeseen access to
answer one big important question. What
is the range? I feel like we hear about
the range all the time on live streams.
It's like this ominous sort of thing,
person that's in control, but also like
an exclusion zone or something. I don't
quite get it. So, today we're going to
be talking to some Space [music] Force
based guardians who are in charge of
actually keeping the public safe. We'll
be talking to experts on the weather. We
hear about weather scrubs all the time
and the constraints on a [music] rocket
if it's safe to launch. We also hear all
the time about weward boats and planes
and things like that in the range. So,
we'll be learning who's tracking that
and who's in charge of all that stuff.
And lastly, we're going to talk about
the flight termination system and the
team of guardians who are inside of the
mission flight control center, which is
an area so classified we couldn't even
bring our cameras into the room at all
because this is where the range would
send the commands to destroy a rocket in
an emergency with that big red destruct
button. So, let's get started.
This video is made possible thanks to a
partnership with United States Space
Force. If you've ever watched a rocket
launch live, whether in person or
online, you've likely heard the range be
talked about by the hosts, either saying
something like, "The range is green." Or
you might hear them talk about how the
range is not clear or red because of a
weward boat or a plane or perhaps a
storm cloud.
>> Hold hold. boarding launch auto. It's
launch director calling a hold. We have
a red range for a fouled range with a
ship in the hazard area.
>> So, let's break down what the range
actually is. The range is both the
physical location that supports a rocket
launch, but it's more importantly the
range is actually made up of teams of
people who make the decisions on launch
day on whether or not it's safe to
launch. Each launch site has a dedicated
range. Now, I'll just be talking about
the United States here, but when
launching a rocket from Vandenberg Space
Force Base in California, there's the
Space Force's Space Launch Delta 30. At
Kennedy Space Center or Cape Canaveral
Space Force Station in Florida, it's the
Space Force's Space Launch Delta 45.
These two sites make up the vast
majority of launches, but there's three
more in the US that are unique. Launches
from NASA's Wallups site in Virginia has
their own Wallups range operations. The
Pacific Spaceport in Alaska is operated
by the Pacific Spaceport Complex. And
SpaceX's Starbase launch site in Texas
also operates their own private range,
which is managed by SpaceX. Each range
needs to work with multiple authorities,
including the FAA, the Coast Guard,
ground security, and the launch
providers themselves to look over and
help facilitate a rocket launch. The
primary goal is always public safety.
The next goal, mission success. There's
always an inherent risk when launching a
rocket with tons of highly explosive and
sometimes toxic propellant on board.
[music] So, I was curious what factors
go into calculating that risk in order
to know how exactly they keep [music]
the range clear.
[music]
>> Hey, I'm Tim
>> Calvin. Nice to meet you.
>> Hey, nice to meet you. What do you do
out here at Vandenberg Space Force Base?
>> Awesome. So, I'm a Space Force
responsible engineer. uh in essence I
assess risk.
>> So uh let's talk about that that risk is
that actually when we talk about risk in
the sense of like it's a risky thing
like is there a tangible number? How do
you do that? What's that like?
>> It is a number right we need something
to say how risky a situation's going to
be right and numbers are the way to do
it. We have huge pages of calculus that
determine exactly what we're getting
ourselves into for any of our launches.
Uh, so does that mean like literally
what variables are you bringing into
this calculation that that help you
determine whether or not it's safe to
even launch in the first place?
>> A lot. Uh, first of all, you need a lot
of space to get to space, right? Uh, so
everything in the physical area around
where these rockets are launches gets
looked at. You know, your planes, your
trains, your automobiles, your boats,
anything that could be in an area where
it is a higher risk to whoever is in
that spot gets looked at. As well as the
history of the vehicle. You know, how
often are we launching a specific
vehicle? um something that's on its
first launch or very few is going to get
look that different than something
that's on its 400th. Uh it all plays
into that that numbers game for us. And
at the end of the day, we want to say
we're 100% zero risk for a launch, but
that's mathematically impossible,
especially for an engineer. Uh so
really, we want as many nines after that
decimal as possible, right? Five, six,
seven, eight nines in a row before we're
confident enough to go. So leading up to
any rocket's launch, but especially a
new rocket, there's teams of people that
are assessing the risks. This comes down
to the finest details, bolt by bolt,
literally, with the design review of the
rocket. But as a mission is actually
planned out, the trajectory itself
becomes a major factor. Most rockets
follow a fairly similar path to reach
orbit, but each rocket takes a specific
path based on a host of variables,
including its acceleration, its
aerodynamic features, the payload mass,
the staging event, the destination of
the payload, etc., etc. So, once a
trajectory is figured out, the rocket
will have a corridor it needs to stay
within. There is a little wiggle room to
allow for some deviation, but the rocket
will need to not exceed those
boundaries. This corridor is then used
to help determine the exact exclusion
zones of where it is and where it's not
safe for humans to be. Whether we're
talking about humans on the ground or on
a boat or in a plane or even just
private property, remember the exclusion
zone because we will talk about it
often. As a rocket develops more flight
heritage and history, there's a chance
those boundaries can change as the
chances of something going wrong become
statistically verifiable with flight
data. SpaceX actually has a great
article that explains how they've been
able to shrink the hazard areas around
launches and landings. Not only have
they done a ton of ground tests to
really calculate the exact yield of
specific explosions with the propellants
they use, but also they fly the Falcon 9
rocket way more often than any other
rocket currently flying. This ridiculous
flight cadence of the Falcon 9 is one of
the reasons why SpaceX is allowed to fly
to polar orbits out of Florida, which
requires overflying Cuba. This is
something that hadn't been an option
ever since a Thorable rocket failure
rained debris on Cuba and killed a cow
Seriously. But even in these edge cases
like overflying another country, the
range will need to coordinate with the
FAA as well as the State Department to
work out the international
considerations. That's just one of those
little details that might be easily
overlooked. So, a lot of work is done
before a rocket flies to get it
certified. And it will receive a launch
license from the FAA or if they're a
government launch, they'll be issued a
launch license by either the Department
of Defense's range safety protocols or
by NASA's launch services program. I
spoke about this exact thing with
Fireflyy's Justin Folen about what this
process looked like for them leading up
to their first launch. There are so many
different aspects uh on what it takes to
launch a rocket, right? Parts, uh paper,
and people. And that paper, what you're
asking about is a is a very loaded and
big question. There are all sorts of
documents that we have to submit to the
range that show that we're compliant and
that we're following all the rules that
they have given us. We're launching on a
Space Force base. We have to follow
their rules. Uh the rules are set in
place to make sure that we're staying
safe, to make sure that the air above us
is staying safe, that space is staying
safe. Um and there's so many different
rules with regards to how we design our
systems, how we operate our systems. Um
and then making sure that everyone else
is out of our way and that we've got
enough space. Making sure that in a
worst case scenario, if uh something
goes wrong, is everyone going to be
okay? There's all sorts of different
things that it takes to get a green
light from the range um to get us
launching this rocket.
>> So, a rocket will have a license on hand
before launch. And frankly, that's
probably not that exciting. So, now
let's go check out the heart and soul of
the range where the actual action
happens and all the important decisions
are made on launch day. Welcome to the
Western Range Operation Control Center,
otherwise known as Rock. So, inside that
building is kind of when you think of
the range and you hear them maybe
talking about, you know, the range is
clear and all that stuff. All those
decisions are being made in this
building. All right. Unfortunately, this
is probably about all the further I can
take you for now. We have to put all
cameras away. This is actually a highly
classified building. I'm not even sure
if anyone's really ever filmed in here,
to be honest. Uh, so we're going to film
as much as we can, but for now, the
cameras have to go away. There's going
to be some rooms in there that are so
classified we can't have like any
electronics at all, period. Uh, so we're
gonna film what we can and the stuff
that we can't film, I'll I'll let you
know what I learned. So, wish us luck.
>> Once through rigorous security, I first
learned about weather with Mr. Addison
Nichols, a [music] weather officer with
the 30th Operation Support Squadron.
>> Uh, so I'm a launch weather officer
here. Uh, I started uh as a uh uniformed
uh launch weather officer or LWO uh for
short. Um, so I came out here as a as a
captain. So, Air Force officer, now I'm
civilian guardian, which is, uh, really
cool. Um, this particular job, uh, the
Air Force officer elbow positions been
around for a little while. Um, I was
originally stationed at Herbertfield,
Florida. On leave, actually, I got to go
down to Cape Canaveral. Um, and I
witnessed a rocket launch. And at that
point in time, that's when I actually
overheard on the radio that Air Force
officers and uh, are the ones that do
the weather for rocket launches. And I
said,
>> "Air force people get to do that. You
mean I get to do that?" That's awesome.
I said, "So if I ever got a chance to do
that?"
>> Um, I would jump at the chance. And lo
and behold, um, the Vanderberg spot came
open. I jumped at it. And obviously I
love the job. Um, so the crux of what we
do, uh, is everything centered on what
we call lightning launch commit
criteria. Uh so uh in a in a nutshell uh
as the rocket takes off the plume uh
from the exhaust that connects the
rocket to the ground if it's flying
through a unique set of atmospheric
criteria will trigger a lightning
strikes. We'll call triggered lightning
and uh obviously that would be
catastrophic to the vehicle if it
doesn't outright destroy it. It'll
impact the avionics on it. Um tell the
rocket to go somewhere it's not supposed
to go um and that would cause
catastrophic failures. Let me pause on
that real quick and reiterate that one
of the biggest weather considerations
for a rocket launch is actually
lightning strikes on ascent. Have you
ever heard of the termce to ox? Well,
when Apollo 12 took off on November
14th, 1969, it was struck by lightning
twice. This caused malfunctions in the
command module which made the entire
panel light up with errors. Luckily, a
ground controller named John Aaron
recognized the pattern of data failure
from simulations and recommended the
crew try moving the signal conditioning
equipment position switch from its
normal position to auxiliary or ox to
receive power from the secondary power
source. Alan Bean flipped the switch and
it quickly cleared up the signal issues
which allowed the crew to safely
continue on their flight. After Apollo
12, NASA made stricter weather launch
commit criteria to prevent future
incidents. Their new criteria included
not launching within 8 kilometers of
thunderstorms, avoiding electrically
charged clouds, and measuring electric
charge from devices on the ground called
field mills. They added a time delay if
a lightning strike was nearby, and they
would also avoid launching in
precipitation. These were the rules that
were followed up until the launch of
Atlas Centaur 67 carrying a Navy
communication satellite in 1987 when an
Atlas Centaur was struck by lightning 49
seconds into flight and caused a
guidance and navigation error which made
the vehicle yaw hard over leading to the
complete loss of the vehicle and the
mission. This resulted in the
development of a new launch commit
criteria based on weather conditions
which has quite a big list of
constraints. Lightning can no longer be
within 16 km of the pad with delays for
high electric field readings. Rockets
cannot launch into thick clouds with
freezing temperatures or within 8 km of
abnormal atmospheric conditions or 5 km
of thunderstorm debris clouds. There's a
minimum of 6.4 km visibility and 1.8 km
ceiling for radar tracking. New minimum
temperatures were developed per vehicle
as well. And lastly, ground winds of up
to around 55 km an hour at the launchpad
and upper level winds are considered to
ensure mission success. These exact
numbers vary rocket to rocket based on
their size, mass, and capabilities.
Addison was telling me more about the
wind conditions and what things are
considered leading up to a launch. We
have wind towers all over the base. We
have uh low-level Doppler wind profiles
profilers uh at the various locations
that kind of sample the winds and the
low levels. U and then we also release
balloons here. Yeah.
>> So you so you mentioned actually the
different fuel types, right?
>> Uh so if it's a solid rocket booster, um
there's a like a toxic risk if something
were to happen.
>> So the safety office uses our balloons
and uh they're able to determine if
something were to happen with a toxic uh
toxic booster, then where do those toxic
fumes go?
>> Right. Right. Um, if the rock is big
enough and it explodes, there's like an
acoustic blast risk assessment that that
uses our balloon data. Um, a lot of the,
uh, commercial providers out here will
use our balloons to assess loads. So, as
the rocket travels up, they hit max Q.
Um, and depending on how the winds are
at that at that particular altitude, if
they're changing direction too rapidly,
changing speed too rapidly, what we
would call wind shear, or if they're
just outright too strong, then the
stress of the wind on that vehicle at
max Q could exceed what the vehicle's
capable of,
>> right?
>> All from balloons.
>> Wow. Wow. [laughter]
>> So, at Vandenberg, there's a separate
weather station where they gather data
from all the ground stations around the
base and radar and even directly from
satellite data. But Vandenberg also
launches weather balloons twice a day
with additional weather balloons
launched during rocket launch campaigns
for upper level wind data. I actually
had the opportunity to see one of these
weather balloons launch while I was on
base.
>> Nice to meet you. I'm Tim Ables's
weather observer here at Ed Vandenberg
Space Force Base. So I'm going to give
you here introduction here of how we do
the weather balloon launches here during
our simoptics which is twice a day. Uh
this is our weather sun that we use as a
visa sun. uh this has in it uh uh that
we'll send back via GPS to our equipment
inside another building uh readings on
humidity, winds aloft, upper air winds,
also um temperature and dupoint as well.
So we'll send that back to our computers
inside and we can track this all the way
up. Uh these balloons typically go up to
about 100,000 ft before they burst. Um
so and again we do these twice a day.
They're done at over 900 weather
stations around the globe and they're
all released at the same time. Uh 4:00
in the afternoon, 4:00 in the morning.
And what that does, it gives a good
global picture of what the what the
weather is doing around the globe and
the movements and temperature and winds.
This also helps us with our with our
rocket launches too. So they can they
can test to see what the upper air winds
are doing and if it's a go or no go
based on winds. So we want to take this
And what I do,
we take this in one hand and the balloon
in the other. What you want to do is
step out here. The winds are from the
northwest
and this is direction southeast. We're
looking out here. So, I'm going to step
out. The winds are going to take the
balloon right away. I'm going to let the
balloon go first and then I'm going to
let the sand go. So, we're going to take
this under the door. It's going to go.
There we go.
>> Holy cow.
Wow, that thing's moving. Okay, so we're
getting some of the pieces of the puzzle
put together. We now know who's tracking
the weather and what exactly they're
looking at. And ultimately, we know the
launch weather officer reports to the
launch director, which we'll get into a
little bit later. So, that's weather in
a nutshell. But what about the exclusion
zone? Well, just a few doors down was
the room where they keep an eye on the
exclusion zone. So, I'm about to go into
the ACC, the area control center. Uh,
unfortunately, we cannot bring our
cameras into this room. Uh, but I'll let
you guys know everything we learn. Okay,
so honestly, the room itself isn't that
exciting. It's basically a room full of
computers and monitors. No surprise
there. It's a relatively small room,
which seems like it would probably have
about five or six people inside during a
launch. Sitting at one station would be
a range safety officer who's in charge
of monitoring air traffic. This person
is specifically called the airspace
safety officer or ASO. Their screen
showed all the air traffic in the area,
similar to what you would see if you
pull up FlightAware or flight radar 24
and you saw all the air traffic. Now,
I'm sure their system is a lot more
sophisticated and much more
comprehensive than those. And they also
use military radar to ensure that the
range is actually clear. The ASO's job
is to make sure that no air traffic is
flying into the exclusion zone, which in
the case of being in a plane, there will
be a section of airspace plotted out
that is active around the time of launch
called a TFR or a temporary flight
restriction. A not TAM or notice to air
missions is published in advance of the
upcoming airspace closure to tell pilots
about the TFR, specifically where and
when they're in place. So each pilot has
to doublech checkck their routes during
flight planning to ensure they're not
violating any TFRs. The other main
console is for marine traffic. This is a
similar thing to air traffic control.
There's a range safety officer called
the marine safety officer or MSO whose
job it is to monitor marine traffic and
they coordinate with the Coast Guard to
ensure there are no boats within the
exclusion zone. Similar to air traffic,
the Coast Guard will publish not Mars or
notice to mariners or sometimes just
called NTMs where there are published
exclusion zones leading up to launches.
As commercial spaceflight operations
ramped up, weward boats and planes were
often the cause of scrubs or delayed
launches. But now, most captains are
more familiar with the possibility of
there being a rocket launch to look out
for when they're planning their routes.
And as you just heard, we did have a
hold hold call on the countdown. That is
because we had a watch item with the
range. Uh we did have a cruise liner uh
making its way towards the no-go zone uh
that the Coast Guard was unable to clear
out uh in time for T0 today. Um but
again, as we mentioned, we do have a
backup opportunity tomorrow. So we will
try one more time tomorrow. Lastly, the
range has to coordinate with Vandenberg
security to ensure there aren't
personnel within the exclusion zone on
the ground. Obviously, when you have an
already secured area like Vandenberg
Space Force Base, there's already a lot
of systems in place to keep people out,
but there's still additional
considerations from range safety
officers, specifically this time, ground
safety officers, who make sure that no
one, including those who work on base,
aren't somewhere that they're not
supposed to be. They'll also put up
additional road closures and blockages
that take effect before prop load begins
on a rocket. All range safety officers
report to a range operations commander
who will be inside the main control room
called the range control center. And
that's the heart of the western range.
And this is where everyone is
communicating with their teams to bring
information up to the launch decision
authority. In this room, I spoke with
one of the range operations commander,
Lieutenant Baronta, who explained to me
one more aspect of range safety that's
unique to Vandenberg.
>> So, trains, fun fact, uh they actually
were here long before Vandenberg Space
Force Base. And so, they actually have
priority over a launch. And so if there
were a train coming through what's
called the train protection area, we'll
have our area surveillance officers
contact those in the train and they can
tell them basically advise them to stop
or if they can keep going. Uh but again
they have priorities. So if they decide
to go then we'd have to adjust our
procedures uh depending on the outcome.
all that information whether something
goes down. If a constraint is violated,
it comes to us and what we do is we take
that information and we provide it to
our launch decision authority who is the
ones that give the authority to launch.
>> Are you talking to the customers too
then or the launch providers like are
you having to talk to to both sides of
the coin in that sense or are you
primarily only uh talking to Yeah. Like
who who are you actually talking to like
on console? Yeah. So, uh, we talk to
obviously the launch decision authority
as well as those that operate work here
in the range, but we are also talking to
the customer. Um, and so for depending
on the launch and depending on the
customer, they have a different title or
role um, whether it's the launch
conductor um, or not. But yes, we serve
as that interface between the customer
as well as the range.
>> There's one more big picture thing
leading up to the launch. This entire
room is full of people listening to all
different pieces of information and
communicating that up to the launch
decision authority or LDA. The LDA is
the person actually in charge of the
launch. They have the ultimate authority
to determine if it is safe to launch,
but they rely on the information being
fed to them from range operations
commanders like Lieutenant Baron.
Another range operations commander,
Lieutenant Rocket, yes, that's his real
name, gave me a little more insight on
what all is going on the minutes leading
up to a rocket launch.
>> There's different uh tracking stations
around this base that is watching the
rocket. So, if we had a base asset goes
down, maybe that rocket is completely
fine. But a tracking station or a
station that works with the flight
termination system, maybe that goes
down. That would create an unsafe
environment. maybe we couldn't see that
rocket or we didn't have enough fidelity
on it. So, that would be another example
where we would have to cancel the launch
or hold the launch or go into an
emergency hold.
>> So, someone might tell you, "Oh, we just
lost our radar tracking dish or
something." And then it's your job then
to communicate that up the chain to say,
"Hey, we have a dish down."
>> Yeah. And there's different steps,
there's different gates in the launch
where we need to know like, "Hey, if
this radar goes down at the three-hour
mark, we can talk about it. What's the
impact?" But if this radar goes down at
the 2C mark, what do we do? Do we have
to do an emergency hold and it's my job
as well as uh the elbow and the safety
officer to say, "Hey, that's not a
problem. We don't have enough time.
Launch the rocket or we do have enough
time. Cancel the launch. Emergency hold.
Take a step back. Let's and make sure
everything is safe." Is do you have the
authority and a physical button to to
pause a launch uh over overriding like
the a launch provider even or is it all
always filter into the like the launch
director at a at a launch provider?
>> It that's a tough question because we
launch so many different vehicles out
here that it's different vehicle to
vehicle. But a standard launch is the
vehicle is on an automated launch like
system and we'll call a red range over a
countdown net where everyone's listening
and you'll have an engineer cancel that
automated launch sequence.
>> Okay. So it is so there is a little bit
of a so if it's 2us 2 seconds there's a
bit of a a rush to all of a sudden go
and no one uh at the range necessarily
has a red button to say pause the launch
though. It has to make its way to uh
>> exactly someone over the nets listening
on countdown net. So yeah, that that's a
big determination for us. So we'll have
different steps. For example, we have
something called the 45se secondond
rule. So once you're 45 seconds from
launch,
>> all of our constraints are removed
except for the absolute mandatory safety
constraints. Something that if we didn't
have would cause a massive safety issue.
>> Also, real quick, you might hear the
word nets be used often when talking
about the communication network. So,
someone might be referring to hearing it
over the nets or something like that,
and that's nets for network. The range
also will conduct a go no-go poll at
some point leading up to the launch.
When and what exactly that looks like
varies from rocket to rocket. After the
go no-go poll, there's a rule that if
anyone says hold hold over the nets, the
countdown is immediately cancelled. Now,
this is different from a single hold
that you might hear where a team will
pause the launch countdown and they'll
try to work the issue. A hold hold hold
typically means cancel everything. We're
not launching today. So if you hear
that, that's bad. It can be anything
from a mechanical issue on the rocket or
on the ground systems or a range
violation or weather. I'm sure most of
us watching this video have been
watching a rocket launch live and we
heard those dreaded words come over the
radio.
>> Hold, hold, hold. Boarding launch auto.
>> So that's a lot of the stuff leading up
to the launch. But now the big question,
what's actually happening during the
launch? For this, I went across the hall
to a room called the mission flight
control center. This is the room where
the termination button is. And in fact,
it's so classified, we couldn't even
bring our cameras into this room at all.
Within this room, teams monitor the
rocket launch. And there's one person
called the mission flight control
officer, or MIFCO, who's ultimately in
charge of terminating the rocket if it
veers off course. Although we couldn't
bring our cameras into that room, I can
give you a sense of what the terminal
looks like that the MIFCO is operating
from and what they're actually looking
at. The station has a handful of video
monitors of the rocket. There's a few
clocks and there's a few just regular
old computer screens. First off, the
officer has video feeds of the launch
from a handful of optical tracking
assets from around the base. Some of the
cameras are infrared, so they are able
to maintain a visual on the rocket
through clouds. Another thing of
interest to me was the fact that the
operator has a foot switch that they
press to open up their communications
instead of a push button. This allows
them to be able to talk on the radio and
be hands-free for other tasks. I just
thought that was kind of cool. But the
main screen they're probably looking at
most is a screen that shows the
trajectory line that's continually
plotted out as the rocket accelerates.
Along with the line, the computer is
also calculating two bounds. One is that
termination line, that kind of 3D bound
that follows the inverted cone shaped we
talked about earlier. If the rocket
crosses that line, it will need to be
terminated. This bound is predetermined
so that no debris crosses the other
bound called the debris impact zone.
These bounds are being calculated and
include all the variables such as
velocity and the heading of the rocket,
the materials of the rocket, and even
the wind and other external variables.
That trajectory line kind of looks a
little bit like Kerbal Space Program
where you can see the trajectory of the
rocket as it ascends. It's obviously a
lot more sophisticated than Kerbal Space
Program and it's taking in a ton of data
from all over the place. And now perhaps
the most important thing at this
station, there's a set of buttons
directly in front of the operator that
says arm and another that says destruct.
So what actually happens when they hit
the destruct button? Well, there's a few
methods of flight termination. Contrary
to popular belief, flight termination
systems aren't necessarily just a big
bomb that's supposed to grenade a rocket
into a million pieces. The main goal is
to simply stop thrust. If you stop
thrust, the rocket will follow a
ballistic trajectory, and as long as
it's within the bounds we just talked
about, it'll impact the ground safely
within the debris impact zone. So, one
of the simplest methods of flight
termination is just a system that
terminates thrust. This can be a manual
override to the engines that simply shut
them down. We especially see this on
smaller rockets where the total
potential blast from impact wouldn't be
too big. A good example of an engine
termination system is Astra's rocket 3.
You can tell when the engines get cut
off and then all of a sudden just falls
back down to the ground. Another good
one was ESR's first launch of their
Spectrum rocket that also had this
incredible shot where you see the rocket
begin to veer off course. The engines
are terminated and it falls right back
down intact. Now, luckily in this case,
the rocket didn't land back on the
launchpad, but it sure was close. Solid
rocket motors also have thrust
termination ports, which are explosive
charges that will blow open ports or
vents in the motor casing to reduce the
chamber pressure and stop thrust.
Another common method for flight
termination are linear-shaped charges.
These are long ribbon-like cords that
are typically mounted on the outside of
the rocket. This helps to ensure the
tanks are completely sliced, so it
splits open and pressure is lost, which
cuts propellant feeding the engines. In
2015, for the CRS7 mission, we saw the
in-flight abort of a Falcon 9 be
triggered. Its linear shaped charges
were activated after the second stage
had an issue on ascent. Lastly, there's
just high explosives charges, basically
C4 placed in strategic places on the
rocket. They're often mounted at common
bulkheads between oxidizer and fuel
tanks to ensure mixing of the
propellants to burn off as much as
possible before it impacts the ground.
Starship famously had two week of high
explosive charges on the first launch
which actually activated when the rocket
was out of control on ascent. The flight
termination was successfully triggered.
However, the charges just blew holes in
the side of the rocket and it continued
to spiral out of control intact.
Needless to say, for flight 2, they
beefed up the flight termination system
a lot, so something like this could
never happen again. Speaking of having
the termination delayed, you might
notice in some videos that it seems like
the Mythco is asleep or something.
They're waiting way too long to
terminate the rocket. Like, there's
clearly an issue and the rocket will
just keep firing and going out of
control and falling for far too long and
the MIFCO is doing nothing. Why? This is
intentional. As long as the rocket isn't
crossing that flight termination line,
the longer the rocket can be
transmitting data back to the ground,
the better. Every single millisecond can
be hundreds or thousands of channels of
data, and these can be used to help look
over afterwards to help discover the
root cause of the issue. Another factor
is if it's safe to do so. Delaying the
termination can minimize the debris
field. If you terminate a rocket several
kilometers in altitude, the debris field
will obviously be much larger than if
you detonate it close to the ground. But
something's changing in this regard.
Rockets have been moving towards an
automated flight termination system or
AFTS since 2017 that is a completely
standalone unit in charge of terminating
a rocket if it goes off course. AFTS is
outfitted with many redundant systems to
ensure the rocket will be terminated
without requiring a human in the loop.
Today's ranges use cuttingedge radar and
even AI to track rockets in real time.
It's a far cry from the 1960s when we
relied on basic telemetry. That said,
the Miffco will still always keep an eye
on the rocket and likely still has an
opportunity to intervene if necessary.
Now that we kind of know how all this
stuff works, I want to explain something

actually pulled up a full replay of

to climb, albeit slower than expected,

about 2 and 1/2 minutes before it

maximum aerodynamic pressure and somehow

cool hearing everyone on comm's talk

be a high stress job in the moment, but
with the right tools and good
communication, it was textbook
execution. Before we wrap up, we
actually have one more vital thing to
talk about. Remember how at the

number one, but mission success is
number two. Well, missions would be
pointless if they weren't successful.
So, that's just as big of a job for the
range. The range is in charge of the
tracking and communication of the rocket
all the way through ascent. Maybe you've
heard talk about ground stations or down

station had a software error that led to

that's when the range activated the

Well, when Apollo 12 took off on November 14th, 1969, it was struck by lightning twice. This caused malfunctions in the command module which made the entire panel light up with errors. Luckily, a ground controller named John Aaron recognized the pattern of data failure from simulations and recommended the crew try moving the signal conditioning equipment position switch from its normal position to auxiliary or ox to receive power from the secondary power source. Alan Bean flipped the switch and it quickly cleared up the signal issues which allowed the crew to safely continue on their flight. After Apollo 12, NASA made stricter weather launch commit criteria to prevent future incidents. Their new criteria included not launching within 8 kilometers of thunderstorms, avoiding electrically charged clouds, and measuring electric charge from devices on the ground called field mills. For this, I went across the hall to a room called the mission flight control center. This is the room where the termination button is. Although we couldn't bring our cameras into that room, I can give you a sense of what the terminal looks like that the MIFCO is operating from and what they're actually looking at. The station has a handful of video monitors of the rocket. There's a few clocks and there's a few just regular old computer screens. First off, the officer has video feeds of the launch from a handful of optical tracking assets from around the base. Some of the cameras are infrared, so they are able to maintain a visual on the rocket through clouds. Along with the line, the computer is also calculating two bounds. One is that termination line, that kind of 3D bound that follows the inverted cone shaped we talked about earlier. If the rocket crosses that line, it will need to be terminated. This bound is predetermined so that no debris crosses the other bound called the debris impact zone. These bounds are being calculated and include all the variables such as velocity and the heading of the rocket, the materials of the rocket, and even the wind and other external variables. That trajectory line kind of looks a little bit like Kerbal Space Program where you can see the trajectory of the rocket as it ascends. And now perhaps the most important thing at this station, there's a set of buttons directly in front of the operator that says arm and another that says destruct. So what actually happens when they hit the destruct button? Well, there's a few methods of flight termination. Contrary to popular belief, flight termination systems aren't necessarily just a big bomb that's supposed to grenade a rocket into a million pieces. The main goal is to simply stop thrust. If you stop thrust, the rocket will follow a ballistic trajectory, and as long as it's within the bounds we just talked about, it'll impact the ground safely within the debris impact zone. So, one of the simplest methods of flight termination is just a system that terminates thrust. This can be a manual override to the engines that simply shut them down. We especially see this on smaller rockets where the total potential blast from impact wouldn't be too big. A good example of an engine termination system is Astra's rocket 3. You can tell when the engines get cut off and then all of a sudden just falls back down to the ground. Another good one was ESR's first launch of their Spectrum rocket that also had this incredible shot where you see the rocket begin to veer off course. The engines are terminated and it falls right back down intact. Now, luckily in this case, the rocket didn't land back on the launchpad, but it sure was close. Solid rocket motors also have thrust termination ports, which are explosive charges that will blow open ports or vents in the motor casing to reduce the chamber pressure and stop thrust. Another common method for flight termination are linear-shaped charges. These are long ribbon-like cords that are typically mounted on the outside of the rocket. This helps to ensure the tanks are completely sliced, so it splits open and pressure is lost, which cuts propellant feeding the engines. In 2015, for the CRS7 mission, we saw the in-flight abort of a Falcon 9 be triggered. Its linear shaped charges were activated after the second stage had an issue on ascent. Lastly, there's just high explosives charges, basically C4 placed in strategic places on the rocket. They're often mounted at common bulkheads between oxidizer and fuel tanks to ensure mixing of the propellants to burn off as much as possible before it impacts the ground. Starship famously had two week of high explosive charges on the first launch which actually activated when the rocket was out of control on ascent. The flight termination was successfully triggered. However, the charges just blew holes in the side of the rocket and it continued to spiral out of control intact. Needless to say, for flight 2, they beefed up the flight termination system a lot, so something like this could never happen again. Speaking of having the termination delayed, you might notice in some videos that it seems like the Mythco is asleep or something. They're waiting way too long to terminate the rocket. Like, there's clearly an issue and the rocket will just keep firing and going out of control and falling for far too long and the MIFCO is doing nothing. Why? This is intentional. As long as the rocket isn't crossing that flight termination line, the longer the rocket can be transmitting data back to the ground, the better. Every single millisecond can be hundreds or thousands of channels of data, and these can be used to help look over afterwards to help discover the root cause of the issue. Another factor is if it's safe to do so. Delaying the termination can minimize the debris field. If you terminate a rocket several kilometers in altitude, the debris field will obviously be much larger than if you detonate it close to the ground. But something's changing in this regard. Rockets have been moving towards an automated flight termination system or AFTS since 2017 that is a completely standalone unit in charge of terminating a rocket if it goes off course. AFTS is outfitted with many redundant systems to ensure the rocket will be terminated without requiring a human in the loop.
